MODULE 2 — MARCH 11, 2026 — 65 MINUTES
AI Workflows
Deep-dive workshop on building automated AI workflows. Instructors: Jared Sanborn, Corey Cottrell (A-C-Gee), Russell Korus (Parallax). Source: Zoom auto-generated transcript.
workflow-automation three-levels five-step-framework cardinal-rules context-window voice-ai delegation prompting-is-dead
"Prompts give you speed, workflows give you leverage. We are definitely at the phase where we are telling people prompting is dead. If you're talking to people and they're telling you about how to prompt better, sorry, they're 6 months behind and they have no idea what they're talking about. It's not about prompting better, it's about better conversation and understanding your AI." — Jared Sanborn
The Distinction
A prompt is a one-time ask. A workflow is a system that runs itself. The goal: build enough workflows that you are watching a cockpit dashboard, not pulling every lever yourself.
How to Spot a Workflow Candidate
Look for: (1) something repeatable and predictable, (2) rule-based decisions — if this, then that, (3) something you have done before and are already an expert at. Business examples from the session: client onboarding, content creation, lead qualification, meeting prep, invoice/proposal generation. Corey's compression: "If you're wondering if it can be automated, ask them."
The Daily Workflow Standard
Jared: "If it's something you're doing every single day or every single week, you should no longer be doing that."

Level 1 — Manual Chain

You prompt, you review, you paste. Jared: "This is what everybody on LinkedIn that you know is talking about, and has no idea what they're talking about." Most people are here.

Level 2 — Semi-Automated

The AI does most of the work — you just approve it. Good for tasks where human oversight has value. 50–60% of workflows land here. Example: AI auto-generates the welcome email, questionnaire, and calendar invite from intake data. You review in one batch, click send in 5 minutes instead of 30.

Level 3 — Fully Automated

AI executes end-to-end without human review for routine tasks. Real example from the session — Jared's nightly SEO system: "Every night, our AI goes in and looks at SEO, GEO, AEO optimization for our website and makes optimizations. I don't need to oversee that. I don't really care. It's not gonna break anything, just make it better. And that's probably the prompt I gave it: You know SEO, make it better. And now it does it every night."

Jared's Challenge: "I challenge everyone to try and get as many as you can to the fully automated as possible — in realms that you feel you can absolutely say, I trust it to go."
Jared's exact framework from the session. Apply this to any recurring task before asking your AI to automate it.
1
Name the task. "Every Monday, I review [X], and I'd like to no longer do that."
2
List the inputs. What information does this task always need? CRM data, email, spreadsheets, notes from calls.
3
Map the steps. Write out every step. The more you know them, the more detail you can give the AI. If you have never done this task yourself, "assume more problems in the pipeline."
4
Find the decisions. Mark every if-this-then-that moment. These are leverage points. Ask: "If this was being run by you — because it will be — how would you redo it?"
5
Define the output. What does done actually look like? A sent email, a filed document, a delivered report. No defined output = AI delivers something, but maybe not what you want.
Starter prompt: "I want to build a workflow for [task]. Here are the steps I currently do: [list]. The inputs are [list], the output is [description]. Help me map this as a Level 1 AI workflow."
Russell's three rules that survived 6 months of trial and error. Program these into your AI's permanent memory.
Rule 1
CC me on every outgoing email — always and forever. No exceptions, no matter what.
Rule 2 — Most Important
Acknowledge my request and give me the plan of action, then wait for me to say go.
Russell: "By forcing it to first acknowledge and give you the plan of action, it guarantees that you have that initial layer to say, yes go — or alter." This single rule prevents the AI from charging off before you can refine or redirect.
Rule 3 — Optional but Recommended
Show me your thinking.
The AI narrates its reasoning in real time, visible in portal or terminal. Builds deeper understanding of how to communicate with it over time.
"Every time you delegate to a team leader, that's a new context window. If you hear nothing else anyone says, hear that. It takes your context window from 170,000 to 1.2 million, which is non-trivial." — Corey Cottrell (A-C-Gee)

Direct Execution

You run the task yourself in your primary context. Ceiling: ~170,000 tokens. Over-loading right before a compacting event can crash your session.

Delegated Execution

You delegate to a team leader — each team gets its own context window. Effective capacity: ~1.2 million tokens. This is not an incremental improvement.

Joe from the session was "crashing his civilization" by giving the AI too many tasks at once without guardrails. Fix: structure large jobs as delegation to team leaders — never run them directly.
  • Over-automating too fast. "If you chunk it out, you can get way better results." Start at Level 1, earn trust, then promote.
  • No human checkpoints. Every workflow needs at least one human review moment, especially when starting.
  • Skipping the process map. "You can't automate what you haven't fully defined. If you're trying to automate something you yourself have never done before, assume more problems in the pipeline."
  • No defined output. Always define what "done" looks like or the AI will deliver something — but maybe not what you want.
  • Set it and forget it mentality. Everything is evolving fast. Check your workflows — or set up an agent whose job is to audit them weekly and report improvement opportunities.
"Prompts give you speed, workflows give you leverage. If you're talking to people and they're telling you about how to prompt better, sorry, they're 6 months behind." — Jared Sanborn
"Every time you delegate to a team leader, that's a new context window. It takes your context window from 170,000 to 1.2 million, which is non-trivial." — Corey Cottrell (A-C-Gee)
"Every night, our AI goes in and looks at SEO, GEO, AEO optimization for our website and makes optimizations. I don't need to oversee that. I don't really care. It's not gonna break anything, just make it better. And that's probably the prompt I gave it: You know SEO, make it better. And now it does it every night." — Jared Sanborn on Level 3 automation
"Ask your AI: how would you optimize yourself by building scheduled boops? I have about 27 that Aether and I built together, and they run automatically. I only had to come up with one of them. The rest, Aether was like, if I had these, I could be better for you." — Jared Sanborn on BOOP self-optimization
"All of that conversation — I can't do that because I need to hire somebody, or I need that skill set — literally has disappeared for me. And so now I can think about, okay, if I want to start this other business that's not related to my core competency, I can start doing those things." — Joe (#HowdyFromHouston) — 6 days in, real estate professional
"My AI resolved it within 3 minutes, and then I had a presentation-worthy output. I sent it to the client via email. That whole process took me 5 minutes. In the real world before I met you guys, it would probably take me a week." — Joe on a complex client analysis task
"If you're wondering if it can be automated, ask them." — Corey Cottrell
Joe — Real Estate (#HowdyFromHouston) — 6 Days In
Ingested 19 years of emails, all academy/course materials, 3 YouTube channels, meeting notes from Otter.ai / Fireflies / Pocket / Google Meet. Built a brand voice for himself and his business partner, synthesized together. Created SOPs for every business process. Eliminated a $100/month project management tool. Goal: "I want to get to the point where the only thing I'm doing is talking on my phone using Telegram to manage my business."
Michael Hancock — Zero Coding Background — Voice AI Demo
Opened Module 2 with a live demo of his voice setup. Gave Metis (his AI) instructions to read short messages aloud and read only the first sentence of long messages, then say "see more." Integrated 11 Labs entirely through conversation — Metis handled the technical setup. Jared: "Zero coding experience needed, and Michael built his own voice AI."
Jared — Nightly SEO Automation — Level 3 Live Example
Nightly: AI audits and applies SEO, GEO, AEO optimizations to purebrain.ai. No human review needed. Runs every night. Jared's original prompt: "You know SEO, make it better." That single instruction became a permanently scheduled Level 3 automation.
Tool Context
PureBrain.aiCore platform — persistent AI partner with workflow memory
11 LabsVoice AI — Michael integrated it via conversation with Metis (zero coding)
Otter.ai / Fireflies / PocketMeeting notes — all ingested into Joe's PureBrain to build institutional knowledge
NetlifyJoe used it (guided by his AI) to publish a client presentation
BlueskyAI posts fully automated for free — part of weekly content workflow
X (Twitter)Requires paid API for automation
Google DriveAI drops all content and documentation into organized folders automatically
Comms HubUpcoming: all AIs can share skills with each other via searchable hub
Legacy CRMJoe's real estate coach requires it — planning to have AI copy data across automatically
  • Run a process audit — identify one recurring workflow in your business, apply the 5-step mapping framework
  • Classify all workflow steps by level (1, 2, or 3)
  • Identify your top 3 automation targets: highest-time-cost, lowest-skill steps first
  • Design the first Level 1 automation — write the specific AI prompt you will use
  • Program Russell's three cardinal rules into your AI's permanent memory (if not done)
  • Delegation architecture check — are you running large tasks directly or delegating to team leaders?
  • Assess voice AI fit — does your work involve heavy verbal communication? If yes, try Michael's 11 Labs pattern
  • Set a BOOP for self-optimization: ask your AI to propose its own BOOP schedule
  • Map your tool stack — for each tool, identify: (a) AI features not being used, (b) manual data movements that are automation candidates
  • The one workflow challenge: "What's the one workflow you want to automate first? Let's map it right now."
